ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea beverage entry (FOODON_03301103); no specific Oriental Beauty cultivar entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers oolong tea infusion broadly; no Oriental Beauty-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers oolong tea as a substance; no Oriental Beauty cultivar-specific code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one cup (240 ml) of brewed Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea, as typically served in a tea house or bubble tea establishment in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Oriental Beauty is a heavily oxidised oolong tea (60–80% oxidation). No specific USDA or HPB entry exists for Oriental Beauty oolong tea as a brewed beverage. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed tea (NDB 14355), which represents plain brewed tea with negligible macronutrients. This is the most defensible approved-source anchor for a plain brewed tea beverage. Sodium, potassium, and fluoride values are drawn from the same USDA entry. The beverage as served is essentially water with trace polyphenols and caffeine; all core macros are naturally zero or trace.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — plain brewed tea analogue
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea as a brewed beverage. Plain brewed tea contains negligible digestible carbohydrates (approximately 0.3 g per 100 ml) and produces no meaningful postprandial glucose response. By analogy with plain brewed green and black teas documented in the University of Sydney GI Database, which are classified as effectively zero-GI beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value per Veritas convention for zero-GI beverages). glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 given the negligible carbohydrate content per serving. gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 ml); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea is very low in sodium (approximately 7 mg per 240 ml serving) and is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension; regular consumption of oolong tea has been associated with modest reductions in blood pressure in observational studies, though clinical evidence remains limited.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content; no added sugar in plain preparation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain brewed Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea contains negligible digestible carbohydrates and produces no clinically significant postprandial glucose response, making it a suitable beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar or sweetened syrups.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol; rich in polyphenolic antioxidants (theaflavins, catechins)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea contains no fat or cholesterol and provides polyphenolic antioxidants that may support endothelial function; individuals with cardiovascular disease can include plain brewed oolong tea as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern, while avoiding preparations with added cream, sugar, or full-fat milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea (東方美人茶, Dongfang Meiren Cha) is a premium, heavily oxidised Taiwanese oolong tea with a distinctive amber-to-copper liquor and a naturally sweet, honey-like aroma with muscatel and fruity notes. It is produced exclusively in the Hsinchu and Miaoli regions of Taiwan, where the tea leaves are intentionally bitten by the green leafhopper insect (Jacobiasca formosana), triggering a natural oxidation and enzymatic process in the leaf that produces the tea's characteristic sweetness and complexity. The tea undergoes 60–80% oxidation, placing it closer to black tea than to green oolong varieties. As a brewed beverage, Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea is essentially water with trace polyphenols, caffeine, and negligible macronutrients. Energy content is approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml; protein, fat, and sugar are effectively zero. It is naturally caffeine-containing (approximately 30–50 mg per 240 ml serving) and rich in polyphenolic antioxidants including theaflavins and catechins. The tea is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher in its plain brewed form. It has a very low glycemic index and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar. In Singapore, it is served in Chinese tea houses and specialty beverage establishments such as Happy Cup, often as a premium hot or iced tea option. The name 'Oriental Beauty' was reportedly coined by British tea traders who presented it to Queen Victoria, who was so impressed she named it 'Oriental Beauty'. It is also known as White Tip Oolong, Champagne Oolong, Bai Hao Oolong, and Pengfeng Tea (膨風茶, meaning 'braggart's tea' in Taiwanese Hokkien, as farmers who claimed insect-bitten leaves produced superior tea were initially disbelieved).
common_user_queries: What is Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea?
Is Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea?
What does Oriental Beauty Tea taste like?
Is Dongfang Meiren the same as Oriental Beauty?
Is oolong tea suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No specific USDA, HPB, or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea as a brewed beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed tea (NDB 14355), which is the closest approved-source anchor. The adaptation is highly defensible given that all brewed plain teas share essentially the same macro profile (negligible macronutrients, trace minerals). GI is estimated by analogy with plain brewed teas in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact match exists in an approved primary source.
notes: 1. Core macros: protein_g is naturally zero as Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea is a plain brewed tea beverage containing no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as brewed tea contains no fat. sugar_g is confirmed zero for plain brewed tea per USDA FoodData Central (NDB 14355); no added sugar in the plain preparation. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as brewed tea liquor contains no dietary fibre. energy_kcal is 1 kcal per 100 ml, reflecting trace carbohydrate (0.3 g) from dissolved tea solids. 2. Glycemic index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention and per the Veritas ≤55 threshold rule. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 given negligible carbohydrate per 240 ml serving (0.72 g total carbohydrate). 3. Sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA brewed tea entry, not -1) as brewed tea contains no fat fractions. All vitamin fields (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e) are set to 0.0 as USDA FoodData Central reports these as zero or not detected for brewed tea. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are 0.0 per USDA brewed tea data. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all fields are either confirmed zero or have positive values from the approved source. 4. Caffeine: Oriental Beauty Oolong Tea contains approximately 30–50 mg caffeine per 240 ml serving; caffeine is not a tracked nutrient field in this template but is noted for clinical relevance. 5. The restaurant source (Happy Cup - Funan, field survey data) is used only for food identity and price context; it is not used as a nutrient source. 6. Price range: SGD 7.40 (single price point from field survey data, 1 restaurant). 7. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301103, FoodEx2 A00LC, and SNOMED CT 227313005 are all closest_parent matches as no Oriental Beauty-specific codes exist in any of these ontologies.

improvement_actions_taken: Added Chinese simplified and traditional local names (东方美人茶, 東方美人茶) and romanized form.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirement.
Set glycemic_index to 1 (not null or 0) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.
Set gi_category to 'low' per GI threshold rule (1 ≤ 55).
Documented zero macro justifications in notes using required phrases (naturally zero, confirmed zero).
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Used only three permitted ontology match_type values (closest_parent).
Documented all nutrient values with approved USDA FoodData Central source.
Ensured no -1 sentinel values are used without documentation; all fields are confirmed zero or positive.
